title: Shattered housing
abstract: Do negative housing shocks lead to persistent changes in household attitudes toward housing and homeownership? We use the residential destruction of Germany during World War II (WWII) as a quasi-experiment and exploit the reasonably exogenous region-by-cohort variation in destruction exposure. We find that WWII-experiencing cohorts from high destruction regions are significantly less likely to be homeowners decades later, controlling for regional differences and household characteristics. Underlying this effect are changes in household attitudes toward homeownership that also extend to preferences for housing consumption, with little or no support for risk preferences, income and wealth effects, or supply-side factors.

article.1. Introduction: Housing is typically the largest component of household wealth. After reaching 69% in the mid-2000s, the homeownership rate in the United States has experienced a significant decline in the following decade, falling to its lowest level in half a century. One explanation for the steep fall in homeownership rates, which is often cited by the popular press and policymakers, is changes in household attitudes toward homeownership fueled by negative housing experiences around the financial crises and the Great Recession (Bracha and Jamison, 2012; Simmons, 2014; Goodman and Mayer, 2018; Heimer and Fritsch, 2020). While this explanation has recently received a great deal of attention, empirically distinguishing its effect from other contemporaneous economic factors, such as stagnant incomes, tight credit conditions, or declining household wealth has proven to be difficult.In this paper, we provide novel empirical evidence on how negative housing experiences can shape long-term household attitudes towards housing and homeownership. Specifically, we use the housing destruction caused by the bombing in Germany during World War II (WWII) as a quasi-experimental framework. This setting allows us to more effectively distinguish the experience effects from other channels, compared to other contexts like foreclosures or other adverse economic shocks. Our results reveal that exposure to extensive housing destruction leads to lower housing market participation decades later, after controlling for regional differences and a broad set of household characteristics. We find that this variation in participation is to a large extent driven by differences in household attitudes toward homeownership. Moreover, we show that exposure to destruction leads to long-lasting changes in consumption preferences, with exposed households opting for smaller dwellings.The WWII destruction in Germany, which leveled approximately 4 million flats and left 13 million Germans homeless (Hampe, 1963), provides a natural setting to study the impact of negative housing experiences on household attitudes and preferences toward homeownership. While the Allied Air Forces bombing led to a total destruction of 22% of Germany's housing stock,1 destruction intensity varied substantially across cities. Exploiting both the variation in destruction rates across German cities and the WWII exposure of different cohorts, we find that a one standard deviation increase in destruction rate leads to an approximately 3 percentage point decrease in the likelihood of homeownership for WWII-experiencing cohorts. We verify the robustness of these findings by performing several additional sensitivity and validity checks such as imposing various restrictions on the sample or using alternative cohort or destruction definitions.Our identification strategy relies on a difference-in-differences type design that exploits the reasonably exogenous regional variation in destruction intensity and cross-cohort variation in exposure to WWII destruction. In particular, we measure the effect of destruction on the differences in housing decisions between war-exposed cohorts and post-war cohorts in heavily destructed areas relative to less destructed areas. This allows us to introduce region × year fixed effects to control for any unobserved (time-varying) regional characteristics.2 Our identification relies on the parallel trends assumption that, had WWII exposure not occurred, the difference in housing decisions between cohorts would be the same in regions with different levels of destruction. We validate this assumption by conducting a falsification test on a sample of immigrants who arrived in Germany after 1955 (i.e., after the per capita housing stock had been restored to pre-war levels) and find no effects.Our nationally-representative household panel data are drawn from the German Socio-Economic Panel (SOEP), covering the period from 1985 to 2017. Since SOEP coverage starts four decades after the end of WWII, we study the long-term effects of the destruction experienced by households who were children or young adults at the time. SOEP includes detailed information on household demographic and financial characteristics such as age, income, education, occupation, and household size. Beyond these variables, SOEP provides a broad set of self-reported “subjective” variables, such as the perceived importance of homeownership. Responses to these questions help us to elicit household attitudes and preferences toward housing, thereby shedding light on the mechanisms underlying observed housing outcomes.It is a-priori not clear how exposure to war destruction would affect household attitudes toward homeownership. On the one hand, a budding literature documents that personal experiences of past economic shocks such as stock market realizations, economic downturns or high inflation have long lasting effects on the economic behavior and choices of individuals (Malmendier and Nagel, 2011, Malmendier and Nagel, 2016; Malmendier and Steiny, 2020; Malmendier et al., 2021).3 Since residential war destruction was a major historical event that represented a significant, direct shock to the housing market, it is reasonable to conjecture that such an experience could lead to negative attitudes toward homeownership. On the other hand, survey evidence shows that many households associate homeownership with a greater sense of security and control, and reasons related to ontological security are key determinants of homeownership decisions (McCabe, 2018).4 Therefore, homeownership may also help exposed cohorts overcome the trauma of war destruction by providing a sense of security, implying that exposure to war destruction may lead to positive attitudes toward homeownership and higher homeownership rates.Our analysis provides strong support for the interpretation that the experience of wartime destruction negatively affects the attitudes of exposed households toward homeownership. Specifically, we first confirm that the response to the importance of homeownership question is a strong determinant of housing tenure and reasonably captures the homeownership preferences of households. We then show that households that experienced wartime destruction express significantly weaker preferences for homeownership decades later, and that the shift in their attitudes toward homeownership is the key underlying mechanism underlying our core findings.The analysis of household attitudes toward homeownership also provides insight into an alternative mechanism: wartime destruction may affect the housing decisions of exposed households by reducing wealth accumulation. If our results were merely a product of unobserved wealth shocks, it is not clear why exposed households would not desire homeownership as much as others, even when facing constraints that prevent them from becoming homeowners. Thus, it is unlikely that our results are simply an artifact of unobserved wealth effects. In addition, we explore several other alternative explanations, including supply-side factors, labor market channels like labor income, unemployment risk, or investment in human capital, differences in risk preferences, and access to credit, and find little or no supportive evidence for them.Interestingly, we also document that the effects of exposure to wartime destruction extend beyond the housing tenure decisions to the housing consumption of households. Specifically, we observe that war-exposed cohorts from highly destructed regions tend to inhabit significantly smaller dwellings, implying weaker preferences for housing consumption many decades later.5 Overall, these results suggest that wartime destruction induces a shift in the attitudes of households toward housing in general, encompassing both its role as an investment and as a consumption good.To gain a deeper understanding of the scope of transmission of experience effects, we examine the impact of wartime destruction on other forms of private property ownership or utilization. Our analysis shows that the effects of (negative) attitudes toward homeownership do not translate into a decreased desire to purchase other goods, nor do they significantly affect the purchases of durable goods such as furniture or vehicles. Furthermore, when we examine the household consumption preferences through subjective questions, we find that households that experienced wartime destruction have a stronger preference for non-housing consumption goods and services, with travel being a notable example.6 Overall, these findings are consistent with previous literature and highlight the domain-specific nature of the influence of past experiences, in our case with a focus on housing (Malmendier and Nagel, 2011; Malmendier and Wachter, 2022).Another avenue of transmission we investigate is the influence of specific housing conditions following WWII. In areas heavily affected by the war, households might have found themselves residing in temporary or government-provided housing, which could have potentially shaped habits favoring renting and a reluctance to become homeowners later in life. To investigate this, we analyze data on early social housing construction in the 1950s, finding no significant impact on the homeownership attitudes or decisions of war-exposed cohorts. These results suggest that government-funded housing facilities and potential habits formed through them are not the primary drivers behind our baseline findings. Furthermore, to differentiate homeownership attitudes from habit-based explanations, we explore ownership of properties not occupied by the owner, specifically real estate held purely for investment purposes. Our analysis suggests that pure real estate ownership exhibits an even stronger negative response to wartime destruction exposure, underscoring the domain-specific nature of these attitudes and dispelling the notion that they stem solely from a preference for renting.The consequences of housing attitudes go beyond the homeownership rate. They also have implications for household wealth and its composition. We find that, on average, the financial wealth of destruction-exposed households is similar to others, but these households have lower total wealth, possibly due to their lower homeownership rate. However, among households with positive total (gross) wealth,7 there is no significant difference between the total gross wealth of destruction-exposed households and others, while destruction-exposed households have significantly higher financial assets. Consequently, their portfolios are tilted away from housing toward financial assets: a one standard deviation increase in war destruction results in a more than 3 percentage point higher share of financial assets for WWII-experiencing cohorts.The potential reasons underlying the unusually low homeownership rate in Germany (44% as of 2010, which was the lowest rate in the European Union at that time) have attracted interest both from academics and policymakers. For example, Kaas et al. (2021) solve a life-cycle model of consumption and housing with endogenous homeownership and attribute the observed low levels of homeownership to high transfer taxes on home purchases, no mortgage interest tax deductibility, and the social housing sector in Germany. Malmendier and Steiny (2020), on the other hand, relate cross-country differences in homeownership rates to past inflation experiences such that the low inflation experience of current German households is consistent with their lower tendency to own a house. We contribute to this discussion by providing a novel additional channel: the widespread destruction Germany experienced during WWII had long-lasting effects on household attitudes toward homeownership, contributing to a lower homeownership rate. Our estimates imply that the homeownership rate of West German natives would be approximately 5 percentage points higher in 1985, the first year of our sample period, in the absence of exposure to war destruction. Furthermore, the declining share of the war-exposed cohort in the population can account for half of the increase in the aggregate homeownership rate since 1985.Our paper also complements a small but growing literature that investigates the long-term effects of warfare on several economic outcomes. These outcomes include but are not limited to health (Akbulut-Yuksel, 2017), socioeconomic status (Kesternich et al., 2014), wealth (Halbmeier and Schröder, 2021), and educational attainment of households (Akbulut-Yuksel, 2014) as well as regional outcomes such as economic development (Brakman et al., 2004; Vonyó, 2012). More broadly, our paper contributes to the burgeoning literature that uses historical natural experiments to provide causal identification of long-run transmission channels that explain present-day financial outcomes (Pascali, 2016; D'Acunto et al., 2019; Levine et al., 2020), labeled as “History & Finance” by D'Acunto (2017).Finally, our finding that exposure to war destruction has a long-lasting effect on household economic behavior relates to the growing literature on the role of past experiences in household economic and financial behavior (Malmendier and Nagel, 2011; Kaustia and Knüpfer, 2008; Haliassos et al., 2020). For example, Kuchler and Zafar (2019) document that past local house price movements influence individuals' expectations of future national house price changes. Similarly, Kindermann et al. (2020) show that homeowners and renters form their expectations about future rents and home prices differently based on their subjective experiences.The remainder of the paper is structured as follows: Section 2 provides some background information on the historical setting that we use in our empirical analysis. In Section 3, we first describe the datasets and then present our identification strategy. Section 4 presents the benchmark estimates, while Section 5 explores the channels of transmission underlying our key results. Section 6 discusses the implications of our findings for the wealth and homeownership trends in Germany, and Section 7 concludes.
article.2. Historical background: 2.1. Residential destruction during WWIIThe majority of German cities experienced widespread destruction through the aerial bombardment by the Allied Air Forces during WWII. Over the course of the bombing period from 1939 until 1945, more than 1.6 million tons of bombs were dropped on German ground that, in total, erased more than 20% of the German housing stock (Diefendorf, 1993). Until early 1942, the British Royal Air Force (RAF) focused its bombing campaign on key industrial targets essential for the German war effort (e.g., metal production plants, military aircraft factories, and oil refineries). This strategy turned out to be ineffective to reduce the German war efforts. The Area Bombing Directive, issued in March 1942 by the British Government's Air Ministry, ordered bombers to attack city centers as new principal targets. The main idea of the new bombing strategy was that the destruction of urban areas would depress the morale of the general population and destroy Germany's will to fight. The US Air Force joined the British RAF in their aerial bombing pursuit. The vast majority of these second-wave attacks were broad area bombardments rather than focused attacks on economic targets.Specifically, the selection of target cities was mainly driven by building density, presence of visible landmarks, and navigational capacity rather than the economic importance of those cities (Friedrich, 2002). For example, Vonyó (2012) finds no statistically significant relation between pre-war industrialization levels and the degree of destruction in a given region. Similarly, historical accounts of the bombardment suggest that wartime destruction also depended on random factors such as weather conditions, pilot errors, and technological malfunctions in addition to the fixed regional characteristics (e.g., proximity to the British airfields) (Akbulut-Yuksel, 2014).Overall, WWII destruction in Germany leveled approximately 4 million flats and left 13 million Germans homeless (Hampe, 1963), providing a natural laboratory to examine the long-run effects of negative housing experiences on household attitudes toward homeownership. Importantly, almost all major German cities suffered to some degree from aerial bombings, though, the intensity of physical destruction varied substantially across regions. Panels A and B of Fig. 1 illustrate the heterogeneity of destruction across West German regions as measured by the percentage of flats destroyed and the accumulated war rubble in m3 per inhabitant, respectively. For example, 60% of the entire housing stock in Cologne was totally destroyed, while this figure accounted for 32% in Munich.Download: Download high-res image (415KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Wartime Destruction in West Germany during WWII. (For interpretation of the colors in the figure(s), the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)2.2. Post-war policiesThe dire housing situation in West Germany in the period following WWII was further aggravated by the arrival of millions of refugees from Eastern German territories and the Soviet Occupation Zone, leading to a housing shortage of around 5 million housing units by 1950 (Wertheimer, 1958).8 Several policies were introduced by the German federal government in an attempt to address the economic and housing challenges. First, the 1948 currency reform exchanged ten Reichsmark for one Deutsche Mark, reducing financial assets and liabilities. This was followed by a substantial levy in 1952, Lastenausgleich or “equalization of burdens act,” which entailed a quasi-flat 50% tax on the net wealth of households and companies as assessed in 1948. The tax was levied on households whose wealth survived the war or benefited from the currency reform, annuitized to be paid over 30 years, and redistributed to refugees and households whose assets were destroyed in the war, albeit many years after the destruction.9 While Lastenausgleich did not provide full compensation for assets lost through war destruction, it had a significant redistributive impact on private wealth in Germany and led to the lowest wealth inequality observed in Germany in the 20th century (Albers et al., 2022).10Second, with the introduction of the social housing laws in the 1950s, Sozialer Wohnungsbau, the government invested in the provision of subsidized housing and construction of new buildings, mostly for renting purposes, which led to a rise in the number of housing units in West Germany and Berlin by around 5 million between 1950 and 1961 (Bundesamt, 1955, Bundesamt, 1964). Most social housing was built by the private sector using subsidized government loans. Units were offered at a below-market rate for a limited time and turned into market-rate housing upon the payment of the loans. With the reduction in housing shortage, the number of units under the social housing program gradually declined.11 Finally, the housing benefit, Wohngeld, was introduced in 1965 to support households that would otherwise not be able to afford housing due to low income or high rents.12 As presented in section 4.4, we ensure the robustness of our results by excluding households living in social housing or receiving housing benefits.2.3. Compensations for war damagesDuring WWII, in order to keep public morale up, the German National Socialist state committed itself to pay for war-induced property damage through the so-called War Damage Decree in 1940. A newly-created war damage ministry (Reichskriegsschädenamt) managed claims and payments related to war damage through war damage offices. While the state promised to pay out the war-damaged, in reality, the law had many loopholes, providing the state with the possibility to postpone or not serve the claims. For example, the timing and priority of compensations were dependent on the national economic development, and minor losses were often denied legal recognition (Hughes, 1999). After 1942, in light of massive destruction due to air raids, the offices had difficulty processing the amount of applications and could no longer pay out (Süß, 2011). By the end of WWII, around one-third of the West German population had lost all or most of their real property. However, claims of the war-damaged could not be legally enforced as the German state ceased to exist (Hughes, 1999), and most damages were not compensated until the arrival of Lastenausgleich payments after 1957 (Albers, 1989).
article.3. Data and empirical strategy: In this section, we first introduce the data sources and provide detailed information on our key variables of interest. We then discuss the econometric challenges in the empirical analysis and explain how we tackle them.3.1. Data and sample constructionWe hand collect information on the percentage of flats destroyed during WWII from the statistical yearbooks of German municipalities (Kästner, 1949) and use it as our preferred measure of WWII destruction in a given region.13 The yearbooks provide information on the Western German municipalities with more than 20,000 inhabitants as of 1946, which accounted for 47% of the historical West German population (Burchardi and Hassan, 2013). Approximately 10% of the municipalities did not report the share of flats destroyed during WWII. Following Kästner (1949), we treat those municipalities with missing destruction information as little- or no-destruction regions. We validate our measure by using auxiliary data on the total tonnage of bombs dropped in each region reported by the British and American air forces from Davis (2006) and set the destruction level of the municipalities with missing information to zero if the tonnage of bombs dropped on a municipality is zero or very low relative to its number of flats.14 We aggregate the municipal destruction rates to the region (Raumordnungsregion) level by calculating the mean percentage of flats destroyed, weighted by the pre-war population of municipalities, to match them to the location of households. Regions are analogous to metropolitan statistical areas in the U.S. but also include rural areas. West Germany is divided geographically into 75 such regions, while the WWII destruction data are available for 73 of these regions.In addition, we make use of the German Socio-Economic Panel (SOEP) (Goebel et al., 2019), which is a nationally representative household panel data, collected annually since 1984. SOEP provides detailed information on demographic and financial characteristics of respondents such as age, education, marital status, place of residence, income, as well as about their attitudes and beliefs. We focus on the sample period from 1985 to 2017 and merge the regional WWII destruction measures with the SOEP data using each respondent's first region location recorded when included in the panel.15In our analysis, we use information at the household rather than the individual level. The full panel of households that completed at least one household, individual, and biographical questionnaire results in 315,608 household-year observations. When constructing the working sample, we adopt a conservative strategy to minimize potential misclassification and measurement errors. Specifically, we apply the following sample selection criteria to arrive at our main sample: (i) household heads must be born in Germany and have German nationality, (ii) household heads born before 1989 must have lived in Western Germany before the fall of the Berlin Wall, (iii) households must be residing in “private residencies” (i.e., not in dorms, nursing homes, or hotels), (iv) renters must pay a monthly rent above 100, (v) households must reside in one of the West German regions with available destruction data when entering the sample, and (vi) household heads must be between 21 and 80 years old at the time of observation. Applying these filters results in 158,009 household-year observations.Table 1 presents summary statistics for the main sample. We winsorize all continuous variables employed in the empirical analysis at the 2.5% level to limit the potential effects of outliers.16 As shown in Panel A, the homeownership rate is 50% in our sample, which is in line with the official statistics. The average size of a household's residence stands at 96 square meters. Additionally, we observe that household net wealth has a mean value of 178,300 while the average gross household wealth is 209,300, suggesting that the average German household has low levels of debt. In Panel B, we see that the destruction of West German regions during WWII was severe. On average, 35% of flats in a given region in Western Germany were totally destroyed. Similarly, the level of variation is also quite high with a standard deviation of 18.5%. Similar conclusions follow when we consider the alternative destruction measure, which is the rubble volume (in cubic meters) per inhabitant. Finally, as presented in Panel C of Table 1, 62% of household heads in our sample are male, have a median age of 51, and have 12 years of schooling on average. Fifty-three percent of households are employed and have an average monthly net income of approximately 2,150. We provide detailed information about the variables employed in the empirical analysis in Section A of the Online Appendix.Table 1. Identification strategyThis section describes our empirical framework to identify the effect of exposure to housing destruction during WWII on households' housing tenure and consumption decisions. We conjecture that the experience of wartime destruction would lead to lower housing market participation and housing consumption in areas with higher levels of destruction. The key econometric challenge we face is potential omitted variable bias when examining the relationship between regional wartime destruction and present day housing outcomes. In particular, there may be unobserved (time-varying) regional characteristics that simultaneously affect the destruction in a given area and the housing choices of households several decades later. For example, destruction during WWII may have influenced economic or financial development in a given region that can have persistent effects today (e.g., local construction cycles or trends in social housing supply).Our empirical strategy addresses this challenge by exploiting regional variation in the intensity of destruction along with cross-cohort variation in exposure to WWII destruction. We measure the effect of destruction on the differences in housing choices between war-exposed cohorts and post-war cohorts in heavily destructed areas relative to less destructed areas. This setting allows us to control for region × year fixed effects, which would capture all (fixed and time-varying) effects of economic, institutional, and housing market characteristics that influence all cohorts in a region to a similar extent. Thus, the identification is based on the assumption that the regional variation in the intensity of destruction is exogenous to the cohorts and their housing decisions. To estimate the average treatment effect of wartime destruction on household outcomes, we estimate the following regression:(1)Yi,r,t=β1⋅Destructionr0+∑c=2Cβc⋅(Destructionr0×Cohorti,c)+αr×αt+αcity+γ′⋅Xi,t+εi,r,t where Yi,r,t represents the outcome of interest of household i in region r at time t. Our main variable of interest is an indicator variable that takes the value of one if household i owns her primary residence in region r at time t, and zero otherwise. We also use the quantity of housing consumed, measured by the size of households' dwellings in square meters, as an outcome variable to assess households' preferences for housing consumption. Destructionr0 is the (population-weighted) percentage of flats destroyed in the region r0 where household i is first observed in the panel. For the ease of economic interpretation, we standardize the destruction variable to have a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. Cohorti,c is a dummy equal to one if the head of household i belongs to cohort c and zero otherwise. In our main analysis, we divide individuals into cohorts based on whether they were directly exposed to WWII, or were born after the war and only experienced the consequences of WWII. We define cohorts born before 1940 as war-exposed cohorts and cohorts born after 1951 as post-war cohorts, which represent our reference category. For cohorts born between 1941 and 1950, the separation between direct exposure to the war and its aftermath is not clear. So, we opt to include this group in the regression and remain agnostic about the effects of destruction for this cohort.αr×αt denotes region × year fixed effects, αcity city (municipality) size fixed effects, and Xi,t a vector of household characteristics, including birth year dummies, gender, education, income, occupation, industry of employment, and marital status controls. We include city size fixed effects to account for any residual unobserved factors related to city size that might be correlated with economic development and level of destruction.17 Detailed variable definitions are provided in Online Appendix A. Standard errors are clustered at the household level to account for correlations in household-level outcomes over time.18The validity of our analysis rests on the parallel trends assumption, that is, the differences in housing outcomes between cohorts would be the same across regions with varying destruction intensity if (exposure to) wartime destruction had not occurred. To test the plausibility of this assumption, we conduct a falsification test focusing on a sample of immigrants who arrived in Germany after WWII. The results (or lack thereof) from this falsification test, as detailed in Section 4.2, support the parallel trend assumption and bolster the credibility of our empirical tests.
article.4. The effects of war destruction on hom: In this section, we first present the results of the baseline analysis of the long-term effects of exposure to wartime destruction on individual housing decisions. We then report the results of a control experiment with a focus on a sample of post-war immigrants to Germany. Finally, we discuss several economic mechanisms through which the experience of housing destruction during World War II affects individual housing decisions.4.1. Baseline resultsWe begin our analysis by estimating the long-term causal effects of exposure to residential destruction on the homeownership outcomes of households. Panel A of Table 2 reports parameter estimates from a linear probability model of homeownership on our destruction measure interacted with the cohort dummies, presented in Equation (1).19Table 2. In column (2), we augment the regression model by incorporating cohort × destruction interactions and a comprehensive set of fixed effects. Regardless of the specification, we document significant negative effects of exposure to residential destruction on housing tenure decisions of households decades after WWII. This negative effect is present after controlling for a rich set of relevant household characteristics such as household income, education, and household size as well as region-time, birth year cohort, and city size fixed effects, as described in Section 3.2. The estimated effects are not only statistically significant; they also bear economic significance. To elucidate, the effect size is analogous to that resulting from a one-standard deviation increase in years of schooling. More precisely, according to the coefficient estimates in our preferred specification in column (2), which account for relevant household characteristics, region × year and city size fixed effects, ceteris paribus, we find that a one standard deviation increase in the destruction rate leads to a 2.7 percentage point decrease in the probability of war-exposed cohorts to own a house (t-stat. = -2.44). This estimate implies a 5.4% decline in the homeownership probability, given that the mean homeownership rate in our sample is 50%.The sign and statistical significance of other controls in our estimation model largely align with earlier estimates for these variables in household finance literature. For example, the income and education level of household heads and household size consistently exhibit positive coefficients in the regressions. Conversely, the indicator variables for living in a rural area and being a male are not precisely estimated. It's worth emphasizing that the introduction of birth year fixed effects in our regressions enables us to isolate the impact of exposure to regional destruction from the broader lifetime economic factors that households encounter, such as stock market returns (Malmendier and Nagel, 2011), inflation (Malmendier and Nagel, 2016; Malmendier and Steiny, 2020), or economic growth, all of which are well-documented as relevant and significant determinants of household economic behavior.Beyond its role as an investment good and asset class, housing also serves as a major consumption good (Piazzesi et al., 2007). Notably, in Germany, housing expenditures constitute approximately one-quarter of net household income.20 Therefore, the war-induced destruction of housing had a dual impact — it not only impacted housing investment returns but also significantly disrupted housing consumption. This impact was keenly felt by the war-exposed cohorts in our sample, most of whom were children at the time, irrespective of whether their families owned or rented their housing units.21 Given this context, we now turn our attention to investigating whether the effects of experiencing housing destruction extend beyond homeownership decisions to influence households' housing (consumption) preferences.22It is worth noting, as a preliminary consideration, that the German rental market is highly developed, with the majority of rental housing in Germany being owned by private households.23 Consequently, the stock of housing units available for rent or ownership is well-integrated, allowing households to find rental or owner-occupied housing in all location and quality segments. This flexibility enables them to adjust their housing consumption, regardless of their choice of tenure.24To assess households' preferences for housing, we consider the quantity of housing consumed, measured by the size of households' dwellings in square meters.25 For a given quality, dwelling size would be the housing input in households' utility function. Controlling for household income and other characteristics, the size of the dwelling thus reveals the strength of the preference for housing.The estimation results are reported in Panel B of Table 2. We find that war-exposed cohorts from destructed regions not only have a lower probability of being homeowners, but also exhibit a weaker preference for housing consumption. Specifically, the regression results show that war-exposed cohorts in destructed areas consume significantly smaller homes (t-stat. = -2.54), suggesting that war destruction led to changes in attitudes toward housing in general, both as an investment and as a consumption good.In Table O.A.3 and O.A.4 of the Online Appendix, we conduct a series of supplementary sensitivity analyses. These include using alternative sample selections, exploring different measures of destruction and cohort categorizations for exposed households, investigating potential non-linearities in the relationship between housing destruction and present-day housing outcomes of households, and conducting additional tests to assess the stability of the statistical results in our baseline analysis against potential downward biases in standard errors. Importantly, all these assessments affirm the validity of our primary findings, underscoring the robustness of our conclusions. A detailed explanation of these supplementary robustness checks can be found in Section B.1 of the Online Appendix.Overall, the results presented in this section suggest that exposure to housing destruction has a “long shadow” adverse effect on both the extensive and intensive housing margins of exposed households. These results align with the increasing body of research highlighting the significant role of individuals' past personal experiences in molding their economic decisions (Malmendier and Nagel, 2011, Malmendier and Nagel, 2016; Malmendier and Steiny, 2020; Malmendier et al., 2021), with these effects outweighing any potential positive sentiments towards homeownership that may arise from the sense of security it may offer to war-exposed cohorts (McCabe, 2018).4.2. Control experiment: immigrant householdsIn our empirical analysis, we identify the long-run effects of wartime destruction by leveraging regional disparities in the severity of housing damage, coupled with variations across cohorts in exposure to WWII destruction. This strategy enables us to incorporate region × year fixed effects, thereby mitigating potential influences from unobserved, possibly time-varying regional characteristics like the level of regional (financial) development. Such characteristics could be simultaneously linked to both the wartime destruction rate and the outcomes for households residing in those areas. Furthermore, our causal analysis hinges on the assumption of parallel trends, suggesting that if WWII exposure had not occurred, the disparities in homeownership rates and housing consumption across cohorts would have remained consistent across regions with differing levels of destruction. To validate this assumption, we conduct a control experiment. Specifically, we focus on the housing tenure and consumption decisions of a sample of first-generation immigrants who arrived in Germany after 1955 (i.e., after the per capita housing stock had been restored to prewar levels), and thus, were not directly exposed to the wartime destruction in their area of residence.The immigrant sample comprises around 40,000 household-year observations. As outlined in Table O.A.5 in the Online Appendix, 43% of the sampled immigrants arrived in Germany prior to 1975, with over 50% of immigrant households having resided in the country for at least 22 years at the time of observation. On average, an immigrant in the sample is 48 years old and has a monthly household income of 2,035, a figure comparable to that of native individuals who are, on average, 52 years old with a monthly household income of 2,150.26In Table 3, we first repeat the baseline regressions of homeownership and housing consumption, as outlined in Equation (1), using the entire immigrant sample. Reassuringly, we find no significant effect of WWII destruction on housing tenure (t-stat. = 0.74) or consumption choices (t-stat. = -0.44) among the pre-1940 immigrant cohorts. This result suggests that the findings presented in Section 4.1 are not a statistical artifact resulting from unobserved cohort differences or (postwar) regional disparities, further affirming the robustness of our underlying assumption.27Table 3. Household attitudes toward homeownershipThe analysis so far shows that the experience of wartime destruction, a major historical event that represents a significant, direct shock to the housing market, has robust, long-lasting effects on households' housing tenure and consumption decisions. These findings are consistent with personal experiences of past events, particularly during formative years, being an important determinant of individuals' economic behavior and choices later in life (Malmendier and Nagel, 2011, Malmendier and Nagel, 2016; Malmendier and Wachter, 2022).This interpretation is clouded, however, by the question of whether our findings might simply be an artifact of differences in wealth accumulation between households in high and low destruction regions. In particular, (unobserved) negative wealth shocks caused by the bombing of Germany during WWII could also lead to lower housing market participation and housing consumption in places with higher levels of destruction, rather than the experience of housing destruction per se. To partially address this concern, we offer an detailed discussion of several tests examining wealth effects in Section B.2 of the Online Appendix. For instance, when we exclude households that report inheriting their current primary residence, receiving a gift or inheritance, or losing a parent due to the war from the sample, and subsequently re-run the regressions for homeownership and housing consumption, we find that the loss of parental wealth and the inheritance channel have little to no impact on our baseline results.29Nevertheless, we acknowledge the possibility that wealth effects may operate through other channels that are unrelated to parental wealth. In response to lingering concerns regarding these potential wealth effects, we adopt an alternative approach that capitalizes on the depth of our dataset, which furnishes information on households' beliefs and preferences, including their attitudes toward homeownership. Specifically, we directly elicit households' homeownership preferences from their responses to a survey question about the importance of being a homeowner. The SOEP administers this attitude question in selected years beginning in 1990. Responses to this question range from 0 (“not at all important”) to 3 (“very important”). Utilizing this ordinal variable, we then construct an indicator variable that takes the value of 1 if the respondent rates the importance of being a homeowner with “very important” or “important” and zero otherwise. Our rationale for this approach is that if the baseline results were driven entirely by unobserved wealth effects, there would be no reason for exposed households not to desire homeownership as much as others, irrespective of any constraints they might encounter that impede them from becoming homeowners.To begin, we verify the validity of our preference measure by investigating whether households' attitudes toward homeownership (Attitude_HO) significantly predict their actual housing tenure decisions. Column (1) of Table 4 presents the estimation results. We find a very strong positive relationship between households' self-reported preferences for homeownership and their observed homeownership status. For example, households that regard homeownership as “very important” or “important” are 44 percentage points more likely to be homeowners, even after controlling for all relevant household and regional characteristics and employing various fixed effects.30 To contextualize this estimate, the effect of the Attitude_HO variable is over four times greater than the impact of being married (in comparison to being single) on the likelihood of homeownership. These results confirm the notion that self-reported attitudes toward homeownership are a very strong determinant of the actual housing tenure behavior of individuals. More importantly, this validation exercise implies that the Attitude_HO variable reasonably captures households' preferences for homeownership.31Table 4. Our results in this section underscore the significance of the preference channel, as we demonstrate that the experience of wartime destruction prompts households to develop adverse attitudes toward homeownership. Consequently, this leads to reduced participation in the housing market and diminished housing consumption in the decades that follow. This mechanism aligns with the theoretical model put forth by Bernheim et al. (2021), which posits that such experiences can have long-lasting effects through shifts in preferences.32 In Section 5, we probe into the nature of various transmission mechanisms to gain a better understanding of the transmission process of experience effects.4.4. Robustness to alternative interpretationsIn this section, we examine other mechanisms, alternative to the attitudes and preferences channel, that can potentially produce similar effects and discuss how we address them.The first mechanism we consider is the influence of supply-side factors. It's imperative to account for potential variations in these factors, such as the ownership structure within the housing market, housing supply dynamics, or subsidies, which could differ between regions with high and low destruction levels. Notably, our estimations consistently incorporate region × year fixed effects, effectively encompassing all unobserved aspects related to a region, including the factors mentioned earlier, that could potentially impact individual housing decisions. Furthermore, our control experiment involving the immigrant sample, as detailed and discussed in Section 4.2, furnishes additional evidence that our results do not simply arise from disparities in supply-side factors across local housing markets. We do not observe any systematic effects among immigrant households of the same cohorts residing in the same regions and sharing the same local environment as native individuals who were directly exposed to wartime destruction. Nevertheless, we continue to conduct additional tests to assess the robustness of our findings in the presence of potential variations in the housing market's ownership structure, as well as the availability of social housing and subsidies to households across regions.Government subsidies, such as Wohngeld (direct payments to low-income households), and the availability of social housing (below-market-rate rental housing) may influence the housing tenure choices of recipients. In fact, as reported in column (1) of Table 5, Panel A, we do not observe a significant effect of wartime destruction exposure on the receipt of rent subsidies. Nevertheless, to address this concern, we proceed to exclude households that report receiving subsidies from our sample and re-estimate the housing tenure and consumption regressions. The results, presented in column (2) of Table 5, Panel A, closely resemble our main specifications in terms of both economic and statistical significance. For instance, we find that a one standard deviation increase in the destruction rate leads to a 3.1 percentage point decrease in the likelihood of war-exposed cohorts becoming homeowners, even after excluding households receiving housing benefits.Table 5. Particularly, stringent rent control policies, if in place, may favor existing tenants and potentially discourage homeownership. Such policies might also distort the rental supply and diminish the quality and quantity of available rental units. However, it's important to note that, after resolving the acute post-war housing shortage, West Germany introduced the Tenure Security Act of 1971 (Wohnraumkündigungsschutzgesetz), which permitted free negotiation of initial rents and rent increases in accordance with comparable properties. This effectively deregulated the rental market (Hubert, 1998). Consequently, housing supply and tenure choices in West Germany were not subject to distortion due to rent control policies. In summary, our findings do not support a significant role for supply-side factors in explaining our results.Another mechanism we explore is the potential impact of wartime destruction on labor market outcomes. It is plausible that residing in an environment with higher wartime destruction could lead to less favorable labor market outcomes, subsequently influencing lower housing market participation and housing consumption. Wartime destruction may introduce additional constraints and obstacles for war-affected households, hindering their capacity to invest in human capital. Moreover, it could directly affect regional labor demand through adverse repercussions on local development.We take several steps to address potential labor market effects. First, we acknowledge that wartime destruction is associated with lower educational attainment among affected cohorts, as evidenced by their reduced years of schooling, as shown in Table O.A.10 of the Online Appendix. This result is consistent with the findings of Akbulut-Yuksel (2014). We also examine the likelihood of obtaining a college education and find significant negative effects of exposure to wartime destruction on this outcome. To further account for labor market effects, we incorporate controls for education, occupation, industry of employment, and labor income, such as earnings or years of schooling, in all our regressions. This approach explicitly considers their influence on households' decisions regarding homeownership and housing consumption.Additionally, we directly estimate the effects of residential destruction on the labor income and unemployment risk of war-exposed households. Strikingly, the regression estimates, presented in Panel B of Table 5, reveal a positive and significant effect of exposure to destruction on the earnings of war-experiencing cohorts, after controlling for other household characteristics. Specifically, a one standard deviation increase in destruction in the region leads to a 2.2% increase in the earnings of the exposed cohort. We also examine unemployment risk and find no significant effects. Furthermore, we investigate whether exposure to wartime destruction affects the skill level of occupations held by war-exposed cohorts, categorizing them into high-skilled and low-skilled. Our results, reported in column (3) of Panel B in Table 5, indicate no significant effects on the skill level of the occupation. In summary, while we acknowledge potential labor market effects, our comprehensive analysis suggests that the labor market is not a prominent explanatory factor for our results.Another mechanism we examine is the role of risk preferences. It is possible that the effects we document operate through household risk preferences rather than preferences for homeownership. Major shocks, such as experiencing a financial crisis, can impact household risk preferences and subsequently influence their willingness to take risks, as demonstrated by Guiso et al. (2018). To explore this alternative explanation, we directly link wartime destruction to various measures of household attitudes toward risk. Following the approach of Dohmen et al. (2012), we use two measures of risk aversion directly elicited through the SOEP survey: the first measures the general willingness to take risks, and the second assesses the willingness to take financial risks. The results, reported in columns (1) and (2) of Panel C of Table 5, reveal no significant effect of wartime destruction on the qualitative indicators of risk preferences. Furthermore, we examine the stock market participation decisions of households in column (3) of Panel C. Once again, we find no significant relationship between exposure to destruction and the actual financial risk-taking behavior of households. Notably, this finding aligns with another key feature of experience effects discussed in the literature, which suggests that these effects are specific to a particular context or domain, as highlighted by Malmendier and Nagel (2011) and Malmendier and Wachter (2022). In our case, the specific domain is housing, as discussed in more detail in Section 5.The final mechanism we investigate relates to the influence of financial institutions. The impact of war destruction on homeownership and housing preferences may be mediated through its effect on access to local financial institutions. Brown et al. (2019) demonstrate that early exposure to financial institutions results in long-term improvements in financial literacy and consumer credit outcomes. Similarly, D'Acunto et al. (2019) have found that households residing in regions with a history of higher antisemitism exhibit a reduced tendency to utilize mortgage financing. Disparities in access to debt could consequently lead to variations in both housing market participation and housing consumption. To explore this potential channel, we examine direct participation in the debt market and find no significant effect on household debt behavior, as detailed in Online Appendix Table O.A.11.
article.5. On the process of transmission: In Section 4.3, we have demonstrated that households who experienced housing destruction during WWII developed negative attitudes toward homeownership. These attitudes, in turn, resulted in decreased rates of homeownership and housing consumption decades later. In this section, we delve deeper into the mechanisms of transmission to gain a better understanding of the scope of transmission and to explore the channels through which it may operate.As we measure the extent of destruction through residential damage, our investigations thus far have focused on the impact of destruction experience on housing consumption and investment. However, it is worth noting that WWII housing destruction may have encompassed not just the loss of the housing stock itself but also a wide range of other privately owned physical assets and objects, whether related or unrelated to housing (such as means of transportation, furniture, and various types of physical property). Therefore, we now turn our attention to examining whether wartime destruction similarly impacts the ownership or utilization of private property beyond housing. If such effects exist, they would suggest that the experience effect of destruction has a broader reach, extending to various forms of private property rather than being confined solely to housing.To test this hypothesis, we first utilize SOEP data to examine whether households replaced furniture that is worn out but still usable with new furniture and whether they own a car. The findings presented in columns (1) and (2) of Table 6 indicate that there are no statistically significant differences in furniture and vehicle purchases between war-exposed households and others. Next, we explore respondents' responses to the question regarding the importance of “being able to afford things for themselves”. This question is posed in the same section of SOEP where attitudes towards homeownership are addressed, as used in Section 4.3 to gauge preferences in relation to homeownership. This new variable would be a direct proxy for respondents' desire to own property in general, including non-housing property. Similar to the question on attitudes towards homeownership, responses to this particular question range from 0 (“not at all important”) to 3 (“very important”). We then construct an indicator variable that takes the value of 1 if the respondent answers this question with “very important” or “important” and zero otherwise. In column (3), we find that war-exposed households tend to place slightly more importance on being able to afford things for themselves, not less.34Table 6. This conclusion aligns with previous evidence indicating that the influence of past experiences is domain-specific. Personal experiences within a particular asset class, such as stock or house price movements, tend to affect expectations or economic behavior only within the same domain. For instance, experiences with stock prices primarily affect stock prices and stock market participation, while experiences related to house price movements primarily influence house price expectations. This domain-specific pattern has been extensively documented in studies such as those by Malmendier and Nagel (2011), Kuchler and Zafar (2019), and Malmendier and Wachter (2022). In our case, the observed adjustments are not indicative of a broad change in attitudes towards property ownership but rather pertain to the specific domain in which a positive or negative outcome has been experienced, namely housing.Another potential avenue of transmission could have been the specific housing conditions following WWII. Following the extensive destruction of housing, numerous households in heavily affected areas may have been compelled to reside in temporary housing or housing facilities provided by the newly established government. These external circumstances might have contributed to a lack of familiarity with owner-occupied housing (or a taste for renting) during the formative years for households exposed to wartime destruction. This lack of habit, rather than direct exposure to housing destruction, could have influenced households' development of negative attitudes towards homeownership, potentially leading them to be reluctant to become homeowners later in life.While the SOEP data lacks detailed information about respondents' childhood environments and habit formation, we explore the potential of this indirect transmission channel to shed light on our findings through various approaches. We demonstrate that exposure to destruction directly influences attitudes towards housing, without extending to the ownership or utilization of other types of property. Moreover, these attitudes cannot be attributed to the potential indirect effect of destruction through the development of a taste for living in rental properties.
article.6. Implications of the findings: 6.1. From homeownership to wealth accumulationThe shift in attitudes toward homeownership can also have implications for the composition and accumulation of wealth. Housing is known to be the primary saving instrument for most households (Goodman and Mayer, 2018), and high realized returns to housing, especially when purchased with leverage, can lead to significant asset accumulation. The findings in a contemporaneous paper by Halbmeier and Schröder (2021) showing that war-exposed cohorts in more destructed regions tend to accumulate less wealth would be consistent with missing out on such effects.A full-fledged analysis of wealth effects is beyond the scope of our study, mainly due to several data-related constraints. First, SOEP starts to collect wealth information in the second half of our sample period beginning from 2002, and this information is only collected at a five-year frequency. This implies that we observe household portfolios only in four out of 33 waves of our data. Second, based on the available data, we observe that around one-tenth of the respondents report having zero gross total wealth, which is defined as the sum of all real and financial assets. We acknowledge that while those responses may reflect the true value of household wealth, some may be due to recall bias (Vissing-Jorgensen, 2002). Keeping these caveats in mind, we provide some interesting indicative evidence on the composition of wealth in Table 9.Table 9. Overall, our findings imply that consequences of housing attitudes extend beyond homeownership and also have implications for household wealth and its composition.6.2. Aggregate homeownershipOur analysis so far shows that exposure to extensive residential destruction results in an economically large reduction in housing market participation decades after experiencing the shock. We came to this conclusion using a difference-in-difference type methodology by comparing the homeownership outcomes of different cohorts in areas that differ in their destruction rates. We can also think about the implications of exposure to destruction for the aggregate homeownership rates in Germany. Given that almost all cities experienced some destruction, exposure may have a sizable impact on aggregate homeownership rates and offer an additional underlying reason for the low historical homeownership rates in Germany (Kaas et al., 2021). Furthermore, the share of war-exposed cohorts in the population has changed significantly over the sample period, so the aggregate effect would reflect this composition change over time.Using the fitted model of column (3) in Table 2 (our main specification), we predict a counterfactual homeownership rate had the war exposure not occurred (i.e., assuming a hypothetical destruction level of zero) and plot it in Panel A of Fig. 2 along with the actual homeownership rate in the data. We calculate that war exposure led to a 3.8 percentage points lower homeownership rate in 1985 compared to our counterfactual of no war destruction. The effect declined to 1.1 percentage points by 2017. The average effect over the years is 2.3 percentage points.39 The decline in the aggregate effect over time is due to the declining share of exposed cohorts, which accounted for 63% of our sample in 1985 but only 8% in 2017. It is informative to compare these magnitudes to the time series of homeownership in West Germany, which increased steadily from 46% in 1985 to 56% in 2017. The declining share of the war-exposed cohort can account for half of the increase in homeownership rate over the same period. Furthermore, Panel B shows that homeownership increased much faster in regions that experienced high destruction (by 11 percentage points from 40% to 51%) compared to the regions that experienced low destruction (by 2 percentage points from 59% to 61%).Download: Download high-res image (343KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. article.7. Conclusion: In this paper, we explore the potential link between negative housing experiences and long-lasting attitude shifts toward housing and homeownership, utilizing the residential destruction resulting from the bombing of Germany during WWII as a quasi-experiment. Exploiting the reasonably exogenous region-by-cohort variation in exposure to destruction, we first document that residential destruction caused by the bombardment of Germany during WWII leads to lower participation in the housing market and lower housing consumption among exposed households decades later. This negative effect is present after controlling for regional differences and a rich set of relevant household characteristics. We verify these findings by performing several additional sensitivity and robustness tests.We investigate the economic mechanisms responsible for the enduring impact of wartime destruction exposure on household housing decisions. Our analysis reveals that variations in homeownership and housing consumption are primarily attributed to shifts in household attitudes. In contrast, alternative explanations, including wealth effects, supply-side influences, labor market dynamics, and household risk preferences, receive minimal or no empirical support. Importantly, the adverse effects observed in homeownership attitudes do not spill over into ownership of other tangible assets, underscoring the domain-specific nature of the war destruction's influence. Furthermore, these effects are not a result of an indirect impact through changes in consumption habits.Exposure to wartime destruction could have had a substantial impact on the overall homeownership rate in Germany, offering insights into the observed low homeownership rates. We estimate that war exposure reduced the homeownership rate in 1985 by about 4 percentage points relative to a counterfactual scenario without wartime destruction. Moreover, the declining presence of the war-exposed cohort since 1985 can account for approximately half of the subsequent increase in the homeownership rate over the next three decades.Finally, it is worth noting that the interpretation of our results requires some nuance, as we study a major historical event that significantly affected the housing market. On the one hand, the effects we document might stem from the catastrophic nature of the shock we study – similar effects may or may not emerge in the case of a less intense shock. On the other hand, the extreme nature of the housing destruction could act as a “magnifying glass” for the experience effects that would also alter household attitudes and decisions, albeit to a lesser extent, in the case of a less severe shock. Both interpretations hold relevance and carry important implications. We remain agnostic about the dominance of one explanation over the other and leave it to future research to determine which of these forces is at play.
